04NHI INTELLIGENCE INSIGHTS

Citywide patterns only visible through simultaneous machine analysis of 500 listings across 27 zip codes

1. THE DC POWER LAW IS WEAKER THAN EXPECTED (R² = 0.60)

DC-wide, Price ~ SqFt0.86 with only R² = 0.60, much lower than Woodley Park’s 0.90. This means 40% of DC price variation is NOT explained by size — it’s driven by location, condition, and neighborhood premium. In Woodley Park, size is the dominant driver; across DC, your zip code matters more than your square footage.

2. THE $340K QUADRANT ARBITRAGE

Upper NW median: $862K at $570/sf. East of River: $432K at $252/sf. Central DC: $700K at $593/sf. The critical NHI insight: Central DC actually has a HIGHER $/sf than Upper NW ($593 vs $570) despite lower total prices — meaning Central DC units are smaller but more expensive per foot. Upper NW delivers more space per dollar than Central DC. East of River is 55% cheaper per sqft than either.

3. ZIP CODE MISPRICING — 5 UNDERVALUED, 11 OVERVALUED

Using the DC-wide power law as a baseline: 20032, 20020, 20019, 20018, 20017 trade 10-25% below expected price for their size. Meanwhile, 20037, 20009, 20005, 20036, 20007, 20003, 20015, 20010, 20001, 20008, 20024 all trade above expected. The NE zips (20017, 20018) represent the strongest arbitrage — well-connected neighborhoods trading at East-of-River pricing.

4. THE “STALE BELT” — GEOGRAPHIC CLUSTERS OF UNSOLD INVENTORY

Stale inventory (100+ DOM) is not randomly distributed. It clusters in: Fort Lincoln (NE), Navy Yard (SE), Wesley Heights (NW), Brentwood (NE), and Central (downtown). These represent micro-markets where supply has overwhelmed demand. Fort Lincoln and Navy Yard are new-construction corridors where developer inventory is sitting unsold — maximum negotiation power for buyers.

5. THE DECADE PREMIUM CURVE IS U-SHAPED

Pre-1920s buildings command a historic premium. 1920s-1960s buildings sit in a value trough. Then prices rise again for 1990s-2020s construction. The sweet spot: 1940s-1960s buildings trade at 25-40% less per sqft than both newer and older stock. These mid-century buildings offer the most square footage per dollar across the entire city.

6. CONDO HOA BURDEN — THE INVISIBLE 20-40% TAX

Across DC, HOA fees represent 0.5% to 4.5% of purchase price annually. The worst-hit zips are older condo corridors: 20008 (Connecticut Ave), 20009 (Columbia Rd), and 20037 (Foggy Bottom). Many buyers compare list prices without factoring HOA, but two condos at the same price can differ by $500-800/month in HOA — equivalent to $100K-150K in additional mortgage capacity.

7. BEDROOM EFFICIENCY INVERTS EAST OF RIVER

In NW and Central DC, $/sqft rises with bedroom count (premium for family-size). But East of River shows the opposite pattern — larger homes are CHEAPER per sqft. This means East of River is the only quadrant where scaling up to 4-5 bedrooms gets you a volume discount. For families needing space on a budget, East of River 4BR+ homes are the mathematical optimum in the entire District.

8. 500-LISTING SUPPLY CONCENTRATION

Of {len(listings)} active listings, the top 5 zip codes hold 47% of all inventory (20009, 20002, 20011, 20001, 20003). This concentration means these zips are in a local buyer’s market even if DC overall appears balanced. Conversely, 20015 and 20007 have only 15-23 listings each — genuine supply scarcity that supports higher pricing.
